items.myth: A module = a file; nothing more to it.
items.reality: A reusable module also has a *public API*, a *shape*, and *import-time discipline*. The file is the easy part.
items.example: Two files with the same line count: one with `__all__`, type hints, no top-level I/O is reusable. The other isn't.

items.reality: Cohesion matters more than file count. A 200-line focused module is friendlier than ten 20-line files that all import each other.
items.example: Splitting `validate.py` into `validate_email.py`, `validate_phone.py`, ... usually hurts discoverability more than it helps.

items.myth: `__init__.py` should re-export everything from every submodule.
items.reality: Re-export only the *curated* public surface; leaking everything makes the top-level namespace noisy and refactors risky.
items.example: `from ragapp import chunk_text, Retriever` ✅ — `from ragapp import _DEFAULT_MAX, _chunk` ❌.

items.myth: Circular imports mean Python is broken.
items.reality: They mean your *modules* are. Two modules depending on each other usually share a concept that wants to live in a third module.
items.example: `models.py` ↔ `db.py` → extract shared types into `schema.py`; both import from it.

items.myth: I'll publish it to PyPI later; modular structure is a packaging concern.
items.reality: Reusability pays *inside the same project* first — across notebooks, pipelines, scripts, tests. Publishing is just a downstream effect.
items.example: Even a never-published internal package benefits from cheap imports and a clear public API.
